---
title: "BFS"
---

## Overview {#overview}

Breadth-First Search (BFS) explores nodes level by level.
In an unweighted graph, BFS returns a path with the minimum number of edges.

BFS is optimal only when all edges have equal cost; otherwise use Dijkstra or A*.

Use BFS when:

- The graph is unweighted (or all edges have equal cost)
- You need a reliable baseline path quickly
- You want a simple reference implementation

## Key Ideas

- Use a queue (FIFO) to expand frontier nodes in layers.
- Track visited nodes to avoid repeated work.
- Store parent pointers to reconstruct the path when goal is reached.
- BFS minimizes number of moves/edges, not physical distance or travel cost.

## BFS vs Dijkstra vs A*

| Algorithm | Edge Costs | Optimal |
|---|---|---|
| BFS | Equal cost only | Yes |
| Dijkstra | Arbitrary nonnegative costs | Yes |
| A* | Arbitrary nonnegative costs + heuristic | Yes (admissible heuristic) |

## Complexity

For graph $G = (V, E)$:

- Time: $O(|V| + |E|)$
- Space: $O(|V|)$ because of the queue and parent/visited structures.

## Grid Connectivity Note

On 8-neighbor grids, be careful with diagonal moves near obstacles.
If your implementation allows diagonal motion, you usually need a corner-cutting rule so the path does not pass unrealistically through blocked cell corners.

## Algorithm Sketch

A high-level BFS workflow can be described as:

1. Initialize a queue with the start node.
2. Mark the start node as visited.
3. Repeatedly pop the oldest node from the queue.
4. If it is the goal, stop and reconstruct the path.
5. Otherwise, inspect each valid neighbor.
6. For each unvisited neighbor, mark it visited, record its parent, and push it to the back of the queue.
7. Continue until the goal is found or the queue becomes empty.

The main idea is to expand the search in layers, which guarantees the fewest-edge path in an unweighted graph.

## Demo {#demo}

The example below runs BFS on a small occupancy grid.
`0` means free cell and `1` means obstacle.

If you want full notebook-style editing and run-any-cell workflow, open:

- [Open In Jupyter Lab](http://localhost:8888/lab/tree/notebooks/path-planning/grid-graph/bfs_demo.ipynb)

Exact steps:

1. Open a terminal at the repository root.
2. Run:

```bash
source .venv/bin/activate
jupyter lab --no-browser --port 8888
```

3. Copy the URL shown by Jupyter (it includes a token) and open it in your browser.
4. In Jupyter Lab, open `notebooks/path-planning/grid-graph/bfs_demo.ipynb` from the file browser.
5. Run cells with Shift+Enter, and edit any cell directly.

Note: `bfs_demo.html` is a rendered static page. The editable environment is the `.ipynb` file in Jupyter Lab or VS Code notebook editor.

## What to Try Next

- Change obstacle layout and test when no path exists.
- Switch to 8-neighbor connectivity and compare path length.
- Compare with Dijkstra and A* on the same grid.
